ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for yaki udon; A00LC (noodle-based dishes) is the closest defensible category anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of chicken yaki udon is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing around 348 kcal, 15.9 g protein, 16.1 g fat, and 35.1 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Yaki Udon — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Yaki Udon (cr_id F05060031). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — udon noodles (cooked) as analogous food
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken yaki udon as a composite dish. GI of 55 is estimated from cooked udon noodles (GI ~55, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021). Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 55 falls in the low category. The protein (5.3 g/100g) and fat (5.35 g/100g) content of the dish are expected to modestly attenuate the glycaemic response relative to plain udon. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (55 × 35.1 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 19.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,299 mg sodium, representing over half the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce, and increase vegetable content to boost potassium intake and partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is moderate at 11.7 g/100 g with low sugar (1.59 g/100 g); udon noodles have a low GI of 55 and co-ingested protein (5.3 g) and fat (5.35 g) further attenuate the postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size to 150–200 g, choose wholemeal or mixed-grain udon where available, and avoid additional sweet teriyaki-style sauces that increase the sugar and glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 433 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is relatively low (~1.2 g/100 g) and the dish contains sesame oil which contributes beneficial PUFA.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should request low-sodium soy sauce, limit portion to 200 g or less, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to improve the overall dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger and garlic provide gingerols and allicin with anti-inflammatory properties; sesame oil contributes PUFA; saturated fat is low at approximately 1.2 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish in moderate portions, minimising soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling and adding broccoli or bok choy to increase anti-inflammatory fibre and antioxidant content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken yaki udon is a Japanese stir-fried noodle dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles tossed with sliced chicken breast or thigh, onion, and a savoury-sweet sauce typically made from soy sauce, mirin, and a small amount of sugar or teriyaki sauce, finished with sesame oil. The dish is cooked at high heat in a wok or flat pan, giving the noodles a lightly caramelised, umami-rich coating. Texture is soft and slightly sticky with tender chicken pieces and lightly wilted onion. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 116 kcal, 5.3 g protein, 5.35 g fat (saturated fat approximately 1.2 g), and 11.7 g carbohydrate with only 1.59 g sugar and 1.3 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 433 mg/100 g, making this dish a moderate-to-high sodium food. The glycaemic index of the udon noodle base is approximately 55 (low by standard classification), moderated further by co-ingested protein and fat. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in controlled portions but requires sodium management for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, and carbohydrate portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes. It is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and food court preparations. The dish is commonly served as a working lunch or light dinner in Singapore Japanese food stalls and food courts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken yaki udon?
Is yaki udon suitable for diabetics?

What is yaki udon made of?
Can I eat yaki udon if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Yaki Udon (cr_id F05060031) at confidence 0.95. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (cooked udon noodles, University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for stir-fried chicken and udon noodle dishes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05060031, and no sufficiently close composite entry was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. 2. Saturated fat of 1.2 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for stir-fried chicken and udon noodle components and is marked as approximate. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on cooked udon noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for chicken yaki udon as a composite dish was found in any approved source. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as low. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match GI value of 55. 4. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 35.1) / 100 ≈ 19.3 g. 5. The input record described the dish as 'western' cuisine, which is incorrect; this has been corrected to 'Japanese' based on the dish's established culinary origin. 6. The input description incorrectly described this as a 'Japanese rice bowl'; this has been corrected to a Japanese stir-fried noodle dish. 7. All core macros (protein_g 5.3, fat_g 5.35, carbohydrate_g 11.7) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 55. Per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of exactly 55 is classified as low.
Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to explicitly state the GI classification boundary and confirm that GI 55 falls in the low category.
Updated type2_diabetes health_context key_factor to reference 'low GI of 55' rather than 'medium GI' for internal consistency.
Updated natural_language_description to reference GI 55 as 'low by standard classification' for consistency.
Updated notes field to document the gi_category correction and the classification boundary rationale.
